segments.paradiso-23-g26: Quale begins a comparative simile ('as' / 'such as'). ne’ is the contracted preposition + article (in + le = in the) marking time/place: 'in the'. plenilunïi is a poetic plural meaning 'full-moon nights' and sereni is an adjective after the noun ('serene/clear'). Trivïa is a proper name (the moon-goddess/Trivia) and ride is present indicative 3rd-singular ('laughs' / 'smiles'); tra + le ninfe etterne places her 'among the eternal nymphs.' che introduces a relative clause; dipingon is an archaic contracted 3rd-person plural present (modern dipingono) 'they paint'; lo ciel is the poetic article + noun order, and per tutti i seni is a figurative expression 'through/over all its bosoms' (seni = folds/expanses of the sky).
segments.paradiso-23-g27: vid’ i’ is an elided, emphatic order for vidi io ('I saw'), with the past tense indicated by the archaic form vid'. sopra migliaia di lucerne means 'above thousands of lamps/lamps (or eyes)' where migliaia is a collective 'multitude of.' un sol is 'one sun' (poetic: single light-source) and che introduces the relative clause; tutte quante l’accendea uses l’ for the feminine plural pronoun 'them' and accendea is the imperfect 3rd-singular (archaic spelling) meaning 'lit' or 'kept lighting'—the imperfect gives a continuous or descriptive tone.
segments.paradiso-23-g28: come fa introduces the comparison 'as [it] does'; fa is present 3rd-singular 'does/makes.' ’l nostro is 'our' with the elided article ('the one of ours'), and le viste superne is the delayed subject 'the higher/heavenly sights' (viste = sights/visions, superne = heavenly/exalted). The clause reads 'as our higher sights do,' with a poetic inversion of normal subject–verb order.
segments.paradiso-23-g29: e per la viva luce trasparea begins with e 'and' + per 'through/by means of'; la viva luce is 'the living/vivid light.' trasparea is the imperfect 3rd-singular of trasparire (archaic spelling) meaning 'showed through' or 'appeared through.' la lucente sustanza is the direct object 'the shining substance/essence,' tanto chiara is an adverbial intensifier 'so/so very clear,' and nel viso mio places that effect 'in my face' (meaning in my sight or in my countenance).
segments.paradiso-23-g30: che is a relative pronoun referring back to 'viso mio' (which), non negates, la is the direct object pronoun 'it' (referring to the shining substance), and sostenea is the imperfect 3rd-singular of sostenere (archaic spelling) 'sustained/held up.' Together: 'which could not sustain it' (i.e. my face could not sustain/withstand it).
segments.paradiso-23-g31: Oh is a vocative interjection addressing Bëatrice; the name with diaeresis (Bëatrice) is the direct vocative and is treated as a proper noun.
segments.paradiso-23-g32: dolce guida e cara are vocative modifiers: dolce ('sweet') and cara ('dear') are adjectives in the feminine singular modifying guida ('guide'); the coordinate construction (adjective + noun + e + adjective) gives two endearing epithets.
segments.paradiso-23-g33: Ella is the subject pronoun 'she' (formal/poetic use rather than verb ending), mi is the indirect object 'to me,' and disse is the passato remoto 3rd-singular 'said'—standard narrative past tense introducing direct speech.
segments.paradiso-23-g34: Quel che ti sobranza is a nominal clause 'that which is left to you' (Quel = 'that, what'; che = relative 'which'; ti sobranza = 'remains to you' with sobranza = 'surplus/that which remains'). è virtù: è is present 'is' and virtù is the predicate noun 'virtue' or powerful faculty. da cui is the preposition + relative pronoun 'from which'; nulla is the subject 'nothing'; si ripara is a reflexive/impersonal present 3rd-singular meaning 'shelters itself / is sheltered'—so 'a virtue from which nothing can shelter itself' (i.e. nothing can be protected from it).
segments.paradiso-23-g35: Quivi is an adverb meaning 'there' (in that place/realm); è is present 'is.' la sapïenza e la possanza are the coordinated subjects 'the wisdom and the power' (possanza is an archaic form of 'potenza' = might). ch’aprì is the contracted form of che aprì: che = 'that' and aprì is passato remoto 3rd-singular 'opened' (it opened/cleared) and le strade tra ’l cielo e la terra is the direct object phrase 'the roads/paths between heaven and earth.'
segments.paradiso-23-g36: onde here functions as 'whence' / 'from which' (classical/poetic connective: 'hence' or 'from which reason'); fu is passato remoto 'was'; già means 'already' / 'formerly' (here 'once'); sì lunga disïanza = 'so long a distance' or 'so great a separation'—disïanza = distance/estrangement. Note: the provided lemma senses for onde are literal 'wave' meanings and do not match this connective use.
segments.paradiso-23-g37: This simile begins with 'Come' = 'As/like'. 'foco di nube' is a poetic image 'a fire of cloud'. 'si diserra' is a reflexive/passive use (si + verb) meaning 'is loosened / parts' or 'opens up'; it describes the fire becoming separated. 'per dilatarsi' gives purpose/result 'in order to expand itself' (infinitive with pronominal reflexive). The result clause 'sì che non vi cape' ('so that it cannot fit there') uses 'sì che' + result and 'vi' is locative 'there'; 'cape' is an archaic form of 'capere' meaning 'to be contained/fit'.
segments.paradiso-23-g38: 'e' = 'and' links the next action. 'fuor di sua natura' = 'outside its nature' (fuor = out, di sua natura = beyond its natural condition). 'in giù' = 'downward'; the verb 's’atterra' is reflexive/impersonal here and means 'it falls/lands down' (Dante uses present-like form to describe the action of the image). The reflexive 's’' marks the intransitive sense 'to fall/come to rest'.
segments.paradiso-23-g39: Short nominative phrase 'la mente mia' = 'my mind' (la = definite article; mia = possessive). 'così' ties this subject back to the simile just given ('thus/so'), preparing the comparison: the mind behaves in the same way as the fire just described.
segments.paradiso-23-g40: 'tra' = 'among' or 'amid'. 'quelle dape' is a compact poetic expression: 'quelle' = 'those (things)', and 'dape' is a substantivized/poetic form related to gifts/graces bestowed (formed from a verb meaning 'to give'); here it identifies the blessings or influences surrounding the mind. 'fatta più grande' is a past participle phrase agreeing with 'la mente' (feminine): 'made greater'. Together: 'among those [gifts], made larger'.
segments.paradiso-23-g41: 'di sé stessa' = 'from itself' (di + reflexive intensifier sé + stessa to emphasize 'itself'). 'uscìo' is an archaic/poetic past form of 'uscire' meaning 'issued forth / went out' — it refers back to 'la mente mia' and indicates that the mind departed from its ordinary center.
segments.paradiso-23-g42: This is a subordinate clause completing the action: 'e che' introduces what follows about the mind. 'si fesse rimembrar' uses the causative 'fare' in subjunctive/imperfect form 'fesse' plus the infinitive 'rimembrar' in a pronominal construction meaning 'to make oneself be remembered' or 'to cause itself to be recalled'; 'si' shows the action refers back to the subject itself. 'non sape' is an archaic imperfect 'did not know' (third person), so the whole clause says the mind 'did not know how to make itself be remembered.'
segments.paradiso-23-g43: 'Apri' is the imperative second person singular 'Open!' addressed to the pilgrim. 'li occhi' is the archaic plural article+noun 'the eyes'. 'e riguarda' = 'and look back / look again' (riguardare can mean 'look at' or 'look back'). 'qual son io' is an inverted interrogative/exclamatory formula 'what / what sort of one I am' (qual = what/which; 'son' = 'I am').
segments.paradiso-23-g44: 'tu hai vedute cose' = 'you have seen things.' 'tu' subject; 'hai' is present perfect auxiliary 'have'; 'vedute' is past participle used nominally as 'seen' (or 'sights, visions'); 'cose' = 'things.' Together: 'you have seen things.'
segments.paradiso-23-g45: The relative clause 'che possente se’ fatto a sostener lo riso mio' modifies 'cose'. 'che' = 'that/which.' 'possente' = 'powerful/capable.' 'se’ fatto' is a contracted form 'sei fatto' (you have become) or 'se’' indicating 'you are' in this register; here it means 'you have become' or 'you are made.' 'a sostener' = 'to sustain/withstand' (a + infinitive to express capacity/purpose) and 'lo riso mio' = 'my laughter/smile' (lo riso = the smile). So: 'which have made you powerful enough to endure my smile.'
segments.paradiso-23-g46: 'Io era come quei' = 'I was like those/one who'. 'che si risente di visïone oblita' — 'si risente' means 'feels again' or 'is affected' (reflexive); 'di visïone oblita' = 'by a forgotten vision.' 'e che s’ingegna indarno di ridurlasi a la mente' — 's’ingegna' = 'applies himself/strives', 'indarno' = 'in vain', and 'di ridurlasi a la mente' is an infinitive phrase 'to bring/restore it to the mind' with reflexive/clitic 'si' referring back to the person. In sum: 'I was like one who, struck again by a forgotten vision, vainly tries to bring it back to mind.'
segments.paradiso-23-g47: 'quand’ io udi’ questa proferta' = 'when I heard this utterance.' 'quand’' = 'quando' (when), 'io udi’' is past tense 'I heard' (udi' = udii), 'questa proferta' = 'this declaration/utterance' (proferta = something offered or spoken). The clause sets the temporal point at which the speaker reacts.
segments.paradiso-23-g48: 'degna di tanto grato' = 'worthy of so great a favor/grace.' 'degna' = worthy, 'di' = of, 'tanto' = so much/so great, 'grato' = pleasing/favor (here 'grace' or 'gift'). The phrase praises the utterance heard as deserving of great grace.
segments.paradiso-23-g49: 'che mai non si stingue del libro che ’l preterito rassegna.' 'che' introduces a relative clause: 'which never is extinguished from the book that recounts the past.' 'mai non si stingue' = 'never is extinguished' (mai = ever/never in negative contexts), 'del libro' = 'from the book', 'che ’l preterito rassegna' = 'that the past reviews/records.' The syntax links the worthiness of the utterance to its permanence in the record of past events.
segments.paradiso-23-g50: 'Se mo sonasser tutte quelle lingue' = 'If now all those tongues were to sound.' 'Se' = if; 'mo' = now; 'sonasser' is an optative/conditional form 'would sound/resound'; 'tutte quelle lingue' = 'all those tongues/languages (voices).' 'che Polimnïa con le suore fero del latte lor dolcissimo più pingue' is a relative clause describing those tongues as belonging to Polymnia (the Muse) with her sisters, nourished by their sweetest, richest milk — a poetic way to say inspired voices. The whole conditional supposes the highest poetic help.
segments.paradiso-23-g51: 'per aiutarmi' = 'to help me.' 'per' expresses purpose 'in order to', and 'aiutarmi' is the infinitive with enclitic object 'me' meaning 'help me.' It completes the conditional thought: even these voices sounding to assist me...
segments.paradiso-23-g52: 'al millesmo del vero non si verria' — 'al millesmo del vero' = 'to the very/utmost truth' (millesmo here = the very vintage/utmost measure, used metaphorically). 'non si verria' is a conditional/imperfect construction meaning 'one would not attain/come' (verriare = to come/arrive; 'si' impersonal). The idea: even with all that help, one would not reach the full truth.
segments.paradiso-23-g53: 'cantando il santo riso e quanto il santo aspetto facea mero;' 'cantando' = 'singing'; 'il santo riso' = 'the holy smile/laughter' (the blessed expression); 'e quanto il santo aspetto facea mero' = 'and all that the holy appearance made plain/clear' (quanto = all that; facea = made/caused; mero = clear/plain/manifest). This enumerates the ineffable things that song would have to render.
segments.paradiso-23-g54: 'e così' = 'and thus/so' — transitional adverb linking the previous conditional to the poet's consequence.
segments.paradiso-23-g55: 'figurando il paradiso' = 'attempting to portray/represent Paradise.' 'figurando' is a gerund/participle 'imagining/depicting', and 'il paradiso' is the object; this introduces the poet's problem in representing heaven.
segments.paradiso-23-g56: 'convien saltar lo sacrato poema' = 'it is necessary to skip the sacred poem.' 'convien' = 'it is necessary/fitting', impersonal; 'saltar' = 'to skip/jump'; 'lo sacrato poema' = 'the sacred poem' (the full, solemn description). The poet says he must omit part of the sacred song because it cannot be adequately described.
segments.paradiso-23-g57: 'come chi trova suo cammin riciso.' This simile: 'like one who finds his path cut off.' 'come' = like; 'chi' = one who; 'trova' = finds; 'suo cammin' = his way/path/journey; 'riciso' = 'cut short' (past participle). The image justifies the abrupt omission: the poet's path forward is interrupted.
segments.paradiso-23-g58: 'Ma' = 'but'. 'Chi pensasse' uses 'chi' as an indefinite relative/pronoun meaning 'whoever' and 'pensasse' is the imperfect subjunctive (protasis: 'if someone were to think/consider'). 'il ponderoso tema' is a noun phrase where 'ponderoso' = 'weighty/important'. 'e l’omero mortal' = 'and the mortal Homer' (l' = article + omero = Homer). 'che se ne carca' is a relative clause: 'che' = 'who', 'se' is reflexive 'himself', 'ne' = 'of it/about it', and 'carca' is present (third singular) of the rare verb 'carcare' = 'to burden/undertake', so 'who takes that upon himself'.
segments.paradiso-23-g59: 'nol' is contraction of 'non + lo' (negative + object pronoun) placed before the verb; here it yields 'would not (blame) him'. 'biasmerebbe' is the conditional mood, third person singular of 'biasmare' = 'would blame/reproach'. 'se' = 'if' (introducing the subordinate condition). 'sott'' is the elided form of 'sotto' used before a vowel and means 'under' (here figurative). 'esso' = 'it' (referring back to the theme), and 'trema' is present indicative 'trembles' — 'if he trembles under it'.
segments.paradiso-23-g60: Word order places the predicate 'non è pareggio da picciola barca' before the relative clause. 'Non è pareggio' = 'is not an equal/match'; 'da picciola barca' = 'for/by a small boat' (da marks the agent/benchmarked standard here). The following relative clause 'quel che fendendo va l’ardita prora' = 'that which the daring prow cleaves as it goes': 'quel che' = 'that which', 'fendendo' is a gerund meaning 'cleaving' modifying the motion, 'va' = 'goes/proceeds' (present), and 'l'ardita prora' = 'the bold/daring prow' (subject within the clause).
segments.paradiso-23-g61: 'né' = 'nor'. 'da nocchier' = 'by a helmsman' (again 'da' marks the counterpart/agent). 'ch’ a sé medesmo parca' is a relative clause: 'ch’' = 'che' = 'who', 'a sé medesmo' = 'toward himself' (reflexive construction with 'sé' and 'medesmo' = 'selfsame'), and 'parca' = adjective 'sparing'/'frugal' (here: cautious or sparing of himself). The clause means 'nor by a helmsman who is sparing of himself.'
segments.paradiso-23-g62: 'Perché' introduces the direct question here and means 'why'. 'la faccia mia' = 'my face' (note the noun 'faccia' with possessive 'mia' after it — normal word order in poetry). 'sì' = adverb 'so/so much'. 't’innamora' is 'innamora' (third person singular present) with the clitic 't'' = 'ti' (you) attached; here 'innamorare' is used transitively 'to make (someone) fall in love' — 'my face so enchants you.' The clitic contraction t' precedes a vowel.
segments.paradiso-23-g63: The clause 'che tu non ti rivolgi al bel giardino...' is a continuation: 'che' = 'that' introducing the content of the question. 'tu non ti rivolgi' = 'you do not turn/you do not turn your attention' (second person singular present indicative; 'rivolgere' here means 'to turn [oneself/one's gaze/attention]'). 'al bel giardino' = 'to the beautiful/fair garden' and the final relative clause 'che sotto i raggi di Cristo s’infiora' = 'which beneath Christ's rays flowers/comes into bloom' — 's’' is the reflexive/pronominal 'si' contracted before a vowel, and 'infiora' = 'blooms / is adorned with flowers.'
segments.paradiso-23-g64: 'Quivi' = 'there' (locative). 'è la rosa' = 'is the rose' (existential 'there is'). 'in che' = 'in which' introduces the relative clause. '’l verbo divino' = 'the divine Word' (the article is elided before the vowel). 'carne si fece' = literally 'made himself flesh' — 'si fece' is the passato remoto (preterite) of 'farsi'/'fare' used reflexively to mean 'became' (i.e., the Word became flesh). The construction is the standard Christological formula.
segments.paradiso-23-g65: 'quivi son li gigli' = 'there are the lilies' ('son' = 'sono' poetic). 'al cui odor' = 'to/whose scent' (relative 'cui' = 'whose'). 'si prese il buon cammino' is an impersonal or reflexive use: 'si prese' (passato remoto of 'prendere' with reflexive pronoun) = 'took'/'began' and 'il buon cammino' = 'the good path' — together 'whose scent set out on the good way' or more naturally 'whose scent took the right path.'
segments.paradiso-23-g66: 'Così Beatrice;' = 'Thus Beatrice;' (short reported-speech tag: these are Beatrice's words or indication that Beatrice has spoken). 'Così' = 'thus/so'.
segments.paradiso-23-g67: 'e io,' = 'and I,' — simple coordinating conjunction plus the first-person subject 'io'. The pause/comma prepares the following relative clause describing 'I'.
segments.paradiso-23-g68: 'che a’ suoi consigli tutto era pronto' is a relative clause modifying 'io': 'che' = 'who', 'a’' is the contracted 'a' + apostrophe before a vowel, 'suoi consigli' = 'her counsels/advice', 'tutto era pronto' = 'was entirely/all ready' (imperfect 'era' used here for state). The whole means 'who was entirely ready for her counsels.'
segments.paradiso-23-g69: 'ancora mi rendei' = 'again I returned' or 'I returned once more' — 'rendei' is passato remoto (first singular) of 'rendere' used here reflexively 'mi rendere(a) a' meaning 'I yielded myself back / returned myself to'. 'a la battaglia de’ debili cigli' = 'to the battle of the weak eyelids' (a poetic way to say he returned to the struggle of keeping his eyes open: 'de’' = 'dei').
segments.paradiso-23-g110: Indi is an adverb meaning 'then' (classical/poetic order). rimaser is an archaic/poetic passato remoto (third person plural) corresponding to modern rimasero, 'they remained'; lì is the locative adverb 'there'; nel is the contraction of in + il 'in the'; mio is the possessive 'my' agreeing with cospetto; cospetto here means 'sight' or 'presence' (literally 'in my sight' = 'in my presence'). The constituent forms a single temporal/location clause: 'Then they remained there in my presence.'
segments.paradiso-23-g111: 'Regina celi' is the title/words of the hymn being sung (Latin phrase treated as the direct object). cantando is the present participle 'singing', here describing the action of the subject from the previous clause; sì is an adverb intensifier 'so' (here 'so/so very'); dolce is an adverb/adjective 'sweet' (here adverbial in sense 'sweetly'). Word order places the sung words before the participle for emphasis: 'singing "Regina celi" so sweetly.'
segments.paradiso-23-g112: che introduces a result/relative clause 'such that/that'. mai ... non together express 'never' (archaic placement: mai often appears with non in older Italian). da is the preposition 'from'; me is the object 'me'; si is the reflexive particle used with partì to form an intransitive/reflexive sense; partì is passato remoto (third singular) of partire 'to depart/leave' (here 'depart/leave'); l' is the article 'the' before a vowel; diletto is the noun 'delight'. The clause literally reads 'that never from me did the delight depart' = 'the delight never left me.'
segments.paradiso-23-g113: This whole segment is a single exclamatory sentence. Oh quanta è l'ubertà = 'Oh, how great is the abundance' (quanta introduces an exclamation and è is the present tense of 'to be'). che si soffolce: che is a relative pronoun 'that', si is the reflexive/impersonal particle, and soffolce is an archaic/poetic verb form (third-person singular) used here figuratively — literally 'is choked/suppressed' but best understood as 'lies enfolded/contained' in this context. in quelle arche ricchissime = 'in those very rich arks/chests' (quelle agrees with arche; ricchissime is a superlative adjective). che fuoro a seminar ... = 'which were used to sow ...' where fuoro is the archaic passato remoto third plural (modern furono) and a + infinitive seminar gives purpose/usage 'for sowing'; qua giù = 'here below'; buone bobolce = 'good bobolce' (bobolce is a diminutive noun; contextually 'little bulbs/seedlings' or 'small morsels').
segments.paradiso-23-g114: Quivi is an adverb 'there'. si vive e gode uses the clitic si to form an impersonal or general statement: 'one lives and one enjoys' (or 'they live and enjoy'); vive and gode are present indicative verbs 'live' and 'enjoy'. del tesoro = 'of the treasure'. che s'acquistò is a relative clause: che = 'that' and s'acquistò is passato remoto (third singular) with the reflexive particle s' used to form a passive/impersonal sense 'was acquired/was gained'. piangendo is a gerund 'by weeping' indicating manner; ne lo essilio = archaic prepositional phrase with ne = 'in' or 'therein' and lo essilio = 'the exile'; di Babillòn names the place/person — here 'Babylon' in the narrative sense (see senses note).
segments.paradiso-23-g115: ove is a relative adverb 'where'. si lasciò is passato remoto third singular of lasciare with the reflexive si; used here in a passive sense 'was left' (literally 'there, the gold left itself' = 'the gold was left there'). l' oro = 'the gold'. This short clause completes the relative clause: '...in the exile of Babylon, where the gold was left.'
segments.paradiso-23-g116: Quivi is 'there'. trïunfa (diaeresis marking two syllables tri-ü-nfa) is the present indicative third person singular of trïunfare 'to triumph' (modern trionfa). The verb stands at the start of a clause that the following phrases specify (who triumphs and under what).
segments.paradiso-23-g117: sotto is the preposition 'under' (can be spatial or 'under the authority of'). l' is the definite article; alto is the adjective 'high/lofty' modifying Filio (archaic spelling of Figlio, 'Son'). di Dio e di Maria specifies possession 'of God and of Mary' — both genitives modifying Filio. The whole prepositional phrase locates the triumph 'under the lofty Son of God and of Mary.'
segments.paradiso-23-g118: di sua vittoria = 'by his victory' or 'of his victory'; di is the preposition 'of/by', sua is the possessive 'his' (referring back to the Son), and vittoria is the noun 'victory'. This phrase explains the cause/ground of the triumph.
segments.paradiso-23-g119: e con l’antico e col novo concilio = 'and with the ancient and with the new council'. e is 'and'; con + l'antico = 'with the ancient' (antico adjectival), col is the contraction of con + il 'with the' used before nuovo concilio; concilio means 'council'. The phrase pairs 'the ancient' and 'the new' councils as companions of the triumph.
segments.paradiso-23-g120: colui che tien le chiavi di tal gloria = 'he who holds the keys of such glory.' colui is the demonstrative pronoun 'that one / he who'; che is the relative pronoun 'who'; tien is an archaic/poetic present-tense form (modern tiene) 'holds/keeps'; le chiavi is the plural noun 'the keys'; di tal gloria = 'of such glory' (di = 'of', tal = 'such', gloria = 'glory'). Grammatically, this relative identifies the triumphant person referred to in the clause.
segments.paradiso-23-g1: Come introduces a simile here and means “like/as.” The article l’ is the elided form of il before a vowel. Augello is a diminutive noun meaning “little bird”/“bird.”
segments.paradiso-23-g2: Intra functions as a prepositional element meaning “within/among” (archaic/poetic usage). L’ is the definite article; amate is an adjective (“beloved”) agreeing with fronde, which here means foliage/leafy boughs.
segments.paradiso-23-g3: Posato is a past participle used adjectivally/verbally: “settled/resting.” Al = a + il (‘on/at the’). Nido is “nest.” De’ is the contracted form of dei/di + i = “of the.” Suoi is the possessive “its/their,” and dolci nati = “sweet-born,” i.e. the young/offspring (nati used as a substantive). La notte begins the relative clause; che introduces the clause “that hides things from us,” with ci = “us/to us” and nasconde = present indicative “hides/conceals.”
segments.paradiso-23-g4: Che is the relative pronoun here, linking the previous noun (notte) to the content clause that follows: “which/that …”.
segments.paradiso-23-g5: Per + infinitive (per veder, per trovar) expresses purpose: “to/ in order to see” and “to find.” Li (article) introduces aspetti, “aspects/appearances/faces,” modified by disïati “longed-for/desired.” Onde introduces the means or source “by/with which,” and pasca is a present subjunctive/indicative form of pascere used here in the sense “feed/nourish” (i.e. ‘with which it may feed them’).
segments.paradiso-23-g6: In che = “in which,” introducing a further comment on that night/effort; gravi qualifies labor as burdensome/weighty. Li is a clitic/dative pronoun meaning “to them,” sono is the present indicative of essere, and aggrati is an archaic/poetic predicate meaning roughly “pleasing/welcome to” or “agreeable to” (here: they take pleasure in such toil).
segments.paradiso-23-g7: Previene is present indicative of prevenire meaning “comes before / anticipates.” Il tempo is the grammatical subject (“the season/time”), in su is a locative phrase meaning “upon/on,” and aperta frasca = “open bough/leafy branch” (aperta modifies frasca). Word order places the action before its location for poetic rhythm.
segments.paradiso-23-g8: E introduces the next coordinated action. Con + noun (con ardente affetto) means “with ardent affection/longing.” The verb aspettare takes a direct object: il sole is what is awaited (“waits for the sun”); the subject (the bird) is understood from the simile context and is omitted.
segments.paradiso-23-g9: Fiso is an adverb/adjectival form meaning “fixedly/intently.” Guardando is a gerund “watching,” and pur (here) means “still/even so” — the phrase expresses continuous, intent watching. Che introduces the content clause “that the dawn may rise,” and nasca is present subjunctive of nascere (expressing hope/wish for the dawn to come).
segments.paradiso-23-g10: Così picks up the simile (“thus/so”). La donna mïa = “my lady” (mïa follows the noun as possessive). Stava is the imperfect of stare and describes a continuing past state: “she stood/was standing.” Etta and attenta are feminine adjectives (“upright” and “attentive”), agreeing with donna.
segments.paradiso-23-g11: Rivolta is a past participle meaning “turned” or “turned toward” (here used adjectivally: ‘turned toward’). Inver’ (inver) is an archaic prepositional/adverbial form meaning “toward”/“in the direction of.” La plaga = “the region/quarter”; sotto la quale introduces the relative clause “under which” and il sol mostra men fretta = “the sun shows less haste” (men = archaic meno).
segments.paradiso-23-g12: Sì che is a resultive connector meaning “so that/with the result that,” introducing the consequence clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-23-g13: Veggendola is an archaic/poetic form (here equivalent to vedendola) meaning “seeing her.” Io is the emphatic subject “I.” Sospesa e vaga are predicates describing the speaker’s state: sospesa = “suspended/held in suspense,” vaga = “vague/uncertain/wandering” (here ‘vacillating’ or ‘absent-minded/faint’).
segments.paradiso-23-g14: Fecimi (fecemi) is passato remoto of fare with enclitic mi: “made me” (i.e. caused me to be). Qual è quei che = “such as is that one who / like one who.” Disïando is a gerund “desiring/longing”; altro vorria uses the conditional vorria (3rd sing.) “would wish/want something else.” The clause describes the state to which the speaker is reduced by seeing her.
segments.paradiso-23-g15: E coordinates the final phrase. Sperando is a gerund “hoping”; s’ is the clitic reflexive pronoun (si) attached to the verb appaga: s’appaga = “is/feels satisfied” or “finds satisfaction.” The sequence expresses the paradox: he would wish something else yet finds satisfaction in hoping.
segments.paradiso-23-g16: Ma = “but.” Poco fu is a laconic way to say “there was little time” or “little time passed” (fu is passato remoto of essere). Tra uno e altro = “between one and another.” Quando = “when.” The clause gives a brief temporal frame.
segments.paradiso-23-g17: Del = di + il, “of the.” Mio = possessive “my.” Attender is the infinitive/noun ‘waiting/expectation’ (here used as a noun: “my waiting”).
segments.paradiso-23-g18: Dico is a parenthetical verb “I say / I mean,” used to clarify the preceding expression (the narrator’s comment).
segments.paradiso-23-g19: E connects the two items the narrator qualifies: del vedere = “of the seeing” (the act of seeing). Lo ciel = “the sky/heaven.” Venir (infinitive) + participle rischiarando (“coming, brightening”) expresses the progressive idea “the heavens coming more and more lit up.” Più e più = “more and more.”
segments.paradiso-23-g20: E = “and.” Bëatrice is the proper name (here the poet’s guide). Disse is passato remoto of dire: “said.” This introduces direct speech.
segments.paradiso-23-g21: Ecco is an interjection “behold/here is.” Le schiere = “the ranks/hosts.” Del trïunfo di Cristo = “of the triumph of Christ.” E tutto ’l frutto ricolto = “and all the fruit gathered” (ricolto is a past participle used adjectivally). Del girar di queste spere = “from the turning of these spheres” (girar = turning/rotation). This is a declarative exclamation describing what appears.
segments.paradiso-23-g22: This segment contains only punctuation (a period) marking the end of the previous speech or sentence break; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.paradiso-23-g23: Pariemi (parve / pariemi) is an archaic 1st-person impersonal form meaning “it seemed to me.” Che introduces the subordinate clause “that ….” ’L (il) suo = “her.” Viso = “face.” Ardesse is imperfect/subjunctive of ardere “to burn/shine,” here describing the face as aglow; tutto = “entirely/whole.”
segments.paradiso-23-g24: E = “and.” Li occhi = “the eyes” (li = plural article). Avea is the imperfect of avere: “had.” Di letizia = “of joy,” and sì pieni = “so full.” The phrase describes the overflowing joy in her eyes.
segments.paradiso-23-g25: Che introduces the result clause: “so that.” Passarmen is a contracted pronominal infinitive ‘passar + me’ meaning “to pass (over) them/me” or “I must pass by,” with the enclitic pronoun. Convien is an impersonal present “it is necessary/one must.” Sanza = archaic for senza = “without.” Costrutto is here an abstract noun meaning “artifice/contrivance/constructed explanation” — the narrator says he must move on without attempting an artificial description.
segments.paradiso-23-g90: Io (subject) + sono (present indicative of essere) is the simple copular clause introducing the predicate noun amore angelico. Amore is the head noun and angelico an adjectival modifier; word order is subject–verb–predicate as in modern Italian.
segments.paradiso-23-g91: che is a relative pronoun ('who/which') referring back to 'amore angelico'; giro is a verb here (‘I turn / I make ... turn’), so the clause means that this Love causes the following thing to circulate. L’alta letizia is a noun phrase ('the lofty joy'), and the following che spira is another relative clause ('that breathes / emanates') modifying letizia; del ventre = 'from the womb'; che fu albergo del nostro disiro is a further relative clause (che + fu, passato, 'which was the lodging/abode of our desire'). The string of relative clauses is typical of Dante’s dense, stacked modification.
segments.paradiso-23-g92: e = 'and'; girerommi is a future form with enclitic mi (girerò + mi) meaning 'I will go/turn about' or 'I will move myself around' — the future tense indicates a future action by the speaker (Love) accompanying the condition introduced next.
segments.paradiso-23-g93: Vocative address: donna del ciel = 'lady (woman) of the heaven' — a respectful title (here addressing Mary). Del ciel is a genitive/epithet: ciel is the archaic form of cielo.
segments.paradiso-23-g94: mentre che = 'while / as long as' (temporal conjunction). Seguirai is second-person singular future indicative 'you will follow/attend to/obey'; tuo figlio = 'your son' (here capitalized in sense as Jesus). This subordinate clause gives the temporal condition during which the speaker (Love) will move.
segments.paradiso-23-g95: e = 'and'; farai is future 2nd-person singular 'you will make/cause/do'; dia is the (archaic) present subjunctive of dare ('give') used in an object/purpose construction with farai — 'you will cause [it] to give' (i.e. to admit or allow); più = 'more'; la spera suprema = 'the supreme sphere' (spera = sfera, sphere of heaven); perché = 'so that' / 'in order that'; lì = 'there'; entre is an archaic form meaning 'enter' (subjunctive), i.e. 'that it may enter there.' In short: 'you will cause the highest sphere to grant more room so that it may enter there.' Note some words are archaic spellings (spera, entre) and the syntax stacks a causative (farai) with a subjunctive (dia/entre) to express enabling.
segments.paradiso-23-g96: Così = 'thus/so'; la circulata melodia = 'the circulated/circling melody' (circulata is a past participle used adjectivally, describing its circular motion); si sigillava is a reflexive/passive imperfect 'was sealed' or 'sealed itself' — the si creates a passive-like sense and sigillava is the imperfect tense, describing the melody being sealed/closed.
segments.paradiso-23-g97: e = 'and'; tutti li altri lumi = 'all the other lights' (lumi = spiritual lights / intellects / celestial bodies); facean is the archaic imperfect of fare ('they made / they caused'); sonare il nome di Maria = 'to sound/utter the name of Mary' — sonare here is transitive 'cause to sound' or 'intoned'. The construction means the other lights intoned Mary's name.
segments.paradiso-23-g98: Lo real manto = 'the royal/real mantle' (Lo = article); di tutti i volumi del mondo = 'of all the volumes/books of the world' — a genitive chain where volumi is used metaphorically (the world's volumes). The noun phrase sets up an epic image.
segments.paradiso-23-g99: che = relative 'which'; più ferve = 'becomes more fervent/seethes more' (ferve used figuratively); e più s’avviva = 'and more enlivens itself / grows more alive' (s’ is reflexive particle, avviva = enliven); ne l’alito di Dio = 'in the breath of God' (ne = in + article l’); e nei costumi = 'and in [human] customs/practices' (costumi = manners, practices). Verbs are present and describe intensification.
segments.paradiso-23-g100: avea = imperfect of avere ('had'); sopra di noi = 'above us'; l’interna riva = 'the inner shore/bank' (riva used metaphoric ally); tanto distante = 'so distant'. The verb + locative phrase describes spatial placement in the past imperfect.
segments.paradiso-23-g101: che = introducing a result/consequence clause 'that'; la sua parvenza = 'its appearance / semblance' (sua agrees with an earlier feminine noun). This begins the clause explaining how distant that inner shore was.
segments.paradiso-23-g102: là dov’ io era = 'there where I was' — dov’ is contracted dove (where), and era is the imperfect of essere ('was'), locating the speaker within that scene.
segments.paradiso-23-g103: ancor non appariva = 'had not yet appeared' (ancor = ancora = yet / still; appariva = imperfect of apparire 'appear' — Dante uses the imperfect to describe something that had not yet become visible to him).
segments.paradiso-23-g104: però = 'therefore / so'; non ebber = 'did not have' (ebber is the passato remoto form of avere for a plural subject); li occhi miei = 'my eyes' (li is the articular form); potenza di seguitar = 'the power to follow' (potenza + infinitive); la coronata fiamma = 'the crowned flame' (coronata adjectival); che si levò appresso sua semenza = relative clause 'which rose up (si levò) near its seed/origin' (appresso = beside/near). The tense and agreement reflect narrative past: the eyes lacked the power to track that flame.
segments.paradiso-23-g105: E come = 'and as'; fantolin = diminutive for small children (poetic); che ’nver’ la mamma = 'who toward the mother' ('’nver’’ = verso / inver = toward); tende le braccia = 'reach / stretch out their arms' (tende = present indicative of tendere used for the children’s motion). The clause is a simile comparing the lights’ movement to that of infants reaching their mother.
segments.paradiso-23-g106: poi che = 'after'; ’l latte prese = 'they took the milk' (prese = passato remoto of prendere) — the temporal clause explains that the reaching happens after they have nursed.
segments.paradiso-23-g107: per l’animo = 'because of / by reason of the spirit/soul'; che ’nfin di fuor s’infiamma = relative clause 'which even on the outside kindles/sets itself aflame' (nfin = 'infin' = even to/unto the very outside; s'infiamma = reflexive/intransitive 'becomes inflamed' in the sense of lively zeal). The clause gives the motivating inner fire for the children's reaching gesture.
segments.paradiso-23-g108: ciascun di quei candori = 'each one of those radiances/whitenesses' (ciascun = ciascuno); in sù si stese = 'stretched itself upward' (si stese = reflexive past form of stendere, describing the extension of each ray); con la sua cima = 'with its summit/top' (cima = tip or peak). The image is of individual luminous points extending upward into a point.
segments.paradiso-23-g109: sì che = 'so that / therefore'; l’alto affetto = 'the high affection / lofty devotion'; ch’elli avieno a Maria = 'which they had toward Mary' (elli = they, avieno = they had); mi fu palese = 'was made plain to me' ('mi' dative, fu palese = became evident to me). The whole clause states the perceiver’s effect: he clearly perceived their devotion to Mary.
segments.paradiso-23-g70: 'Come' introduces a simile ('like/as'); the noun phrase 'a raggio di sol' = 'a ray of the sun' (Dante uses the archaic 'sol' for 'sole'). Word order is the simple simile head and prepositional phrase; 'a' is the preposition linking 'raggio' to the simile.
segments.paradiso-23-g71: This is a relative clause ('che' = 'which') modifying 'raggio'. 'puro' = 'clear/pure' qualifies the ray; 'mei' is an archaic form connected to 'li occhi miei' later (poetic placement of the possessive), effectively 'to my [eyes]'; 'per' = 'through' and 'fratta nube' = 'a rent/broken cloud' (an idiomatic, somewhat archaic compound).
segments.paradiso-23-g72: 'già' is an adverb 'already'; 'prato di fiori' is a noun phrase 'meadow of flowers'; 'vider' is the archaic past infinitive/remote past form of vedere (here meaning 'saw' or 'showed'), placed before its subject for metrical/poetic inversion (the subject follows).
segments.paradiso-23-g73: 'coverti' is a past participle ('covered') agreeing in number with the noun it modifies (in the clause it actually agrees with 'li occhi miei' which follows); 'd’ombra' = 'with shadow/under shade'. The participle is placed before the noun for verse effect.
segments.paradiso-23-g74: The phrase 'li occhi miei' ('the my eyes' = 'my eyes') is the subject of the earlier verb 'vider'; the article 'li' is the archaic plural article (modern 'i'), and the possessive 'miei' follows the noun as normal in Italian.
segments.paradiso-23-g75: 'Vid' io' is the contracted, archaic past ('vidi') with the pronoun 'io' after the verb for emphasis—'I saw.' 'così' = 'thus/so'; 'più turbe di splendori' = 'even more crowds/hosts of splendors' (plural noun 'turbe' + 'di splendori').
segments.paradiso-23-g76: 'folgorate' is a past participle agreeing with 'turbe' (feminine plural) meaning 'struck/dazzled (by light)'; 'di sù' = 'from on high/above' and 'da raggi ardenti' gives the agent/source 'by burning rays.' Note the stacked prepositional phrases showing source and direction.
segments.paradiso-23-g77: 'sanza' is the archaic form of 'senza' ('without'); it is followed by the infinitive 'veder' ('to see'). 'principio di folgóri' = 'the origin/beginning of the flashes/lightning'—the speaker cannot see where the flashes begin.
segments.paradiso-23-g78: 'O' is a vocative interjection. 'benigna vertù' = 'kind/benign Power' (Dante often uses 'vertù' to mean divine power). 'che' introduces the relative clause; 'sì' is an adverb 'so/thus'; 'li' is the plural object pronoun ('them') and '’mprenti' is an archaic contracted form of 'imprenti'/'imprentar' (to imprint/press)—an aphetic form missing an initial vowel.
segments.paradiso-23-g79: 'sù' = 'on high/above' (adverb). 't’ essaltasti' is the 2nd person singular past historic reflexive form 'ti esaltasti' = 'you exalted yourself' (the enclitic 't’' = 'yourself'). The verb is past and emphatic, and the reflexive marks the action done by the Power onto itself (i.e., raising itself up).
segments.paradiso-23-g80: 'per' introduces purpose ('in order to'); 'largirmi' = 'to grant me' (verb + enclitic 'mi' = 'to me'); 'loco' = 'a place/room' (here, figuratively, 'space for sight'). 'a li occhi lì' = 'for those eyes there' (dative with 'a'); the relative clause 'che non t’eran possenti' modifies 'occhi'—'eran' is imperfect 3rd pl 'they were' and 't’' is dative 'to you/for you', so 'which were not powerful enough for you.'
segments.paradiso-23-g81: 'Il nome del bel fior' is the subject ('the name of the fair flower'); 'ch’io sempre invoco' is a relative clause 'that I always invoke' (present indicative 'invoco'). 'e mane e sera' is a correlative 'both morning and evening' (the word 'mane' here is archaic 'morning' and not the lemma 'mano' listed).
segments.paradiso-23-g82: 'tutto' = 'altogether/completely' modifies the action; 'mi ristrinse' is past historic 'narrowed/restrained me' with 'mi' indirect object; 'l’animo ad avvisar lo maggior foco' = 'the spirit to perceive/consider the greater fire' — note the infinitive 'avvisar' (archaic spelling) and that 'ristrinse' takes a noun and an infinitive phrase as its complements.
segments.paradiso-23-g83: The clause 'e come ambo le luci mi dipinse' = 'and as both lights depicted/painted for me' (dipinse = past of dipingere). 'il quale e il quanto' = 'the which and the how much' (abstract qualities); the relative clause describes 'la viva stella' ('the living star'). The pair 'vince... vinse' sets up a parallel: the star 'wins/surpasses' above in the present, as it 'won' here below in the past—Dante uses tenses to contrast celestial and terrestrial loci.
segments.paradiso-23-g84: 'per entro il cielo' = 'through/within the heaven' (a spatial prepositional phrase); 'scese' is the past historic 3rd singular of 'scendere' ('descended'), and 'una facella' (a small torch/torchlet) is the subject placed after the verb for metrical order.
segments.paradiso-23-g85: 'formata' is the past participle modifying 'facella' ('formed/shaped'); 'in cerchio' = 'into a circle'; 'a guisa di corona' = 'in the fashion/likeness of a crown' — a standard simile introduced by 'a guisa di'.
segments.paradiso-23-g86: This short clause uses two rare/uncertain verbs ('cinsela' and 'girossi') surrounding the adverbial 'intorno ad ella' = 'around it'. The structure is 'and [it] cinsela and girossi around it'; both verbs are archaic/obscure in form and are here best read as 'girded/encircled and revolved around it' (Dante's verb choices are elliptical and poetic).
segments.paradiso-23-g87: 'Qualunque melodia' = 'whatever/any melody'; comparative structure 'più dolce... e più ... tira' ('the sweeter ... and the more ... draws') links two comparative ideas. 'qua giù' = 'here below'; 'a sé l’anima tira' literally 'draws the soul to itself' (reflexive 'sé' marks attraction to the melody). The verbs are present tense general statements.
segments.paradiso-23-g88: 'parrebbe' is the conditional ('it would seem'); 'nube che squarciata tona' = 'a cloud which, rent/torn, thunders' — 'squarciata' is a past participle ('torn') modifying 'nube' and 'tona' (from 'tonare') is the verb 'thunders'. The conditional frames the hypothetical comparison (what the earthly melody would seem in comparison).
segments.paradiso-23-g89: 'comparata al sonar di quella lira' = 'compared to the sound of that lyre' (comparata = past participle); 'onde si coronava il bel zaffiro' is a relative clause 'from which/by which the fair sapphire was crowned' (si + coronava gives a passive/reflexive sense). 'del quale il ciel più chiaro s’inzaffira' = 'of which the clearer heaven becomes/encrusted with sapphire' — 's’inzaffira' is a rare/poetic verb meaning to become sapphire-colored or be adorned with sapphire, reflexive/passive in form.

sentences.paradiso-23-s5: As on serene full-moon nights Trivia laughs among the eternal nymphs who paint the sky over all its bosoms, so I saw above thousands of lamps one sun that lit them all, as our higher sights do; and through the living light the shining substance appeared so clear in my face that my face could not sustain it.
sentences.paradiso-23-s6: Oh Beatrice, sweet and dear guide!
sentences.paradiso-23-s7: She said to me: 'What remains to you is a virtue from which nothing can be sheltered.'
sentences.paradiso-23-s8: There is the wisdom and the power that opened the roads between heaven and earth, whence there was once so long a distance.
sentences.paradiso-23-s9: As a fire in a cloud loosens itself to expand so much that it can no longer be contained there, and, outside its nature, falls downward, so my mind, thus made greater among those gifts, went out of itself and did not know how to make itself be remembered.
sentences.paradiso-23-s10: "Open your eyes and look back to see who I am; you have seen things that have made you mighty enough to bear my laughter."
sentences.paradiso-23-s11: I was like one who is affected by a forgotten vision and strives in vain to restore it to his mind, when I heard this utterance, worthy of so great a grace that never is extinguished from the book which recounts the past.
sentences.paradiso-23-s12: If now all those tongues that Polymnia and her fierce sisters once bore, of their milk most sweet and more rich, were to sound to help me, even so, at the very truth, one would not come, singing the holy smile and how the holy aspect made plain; and so, in trying to picture Paradise, the sacred poem must be skipped, like one who finds his path cut short.
sentences.paradiso-23-s13: But whoever were to consider the weighty theme and the mortal Homer who takes it upon himself would not blame him if he trembles beneath it: that which the daring prow cleaves as it goes is not a match for a little boat, nor for a helmsman who is sparing of himself.
sentences.paradiso-23-s14: "Why does my face so enamor you that you do not turn to the fair garden which blossoms beneath the rays of Christ?"
sentences.paradiso-23-s15: There is the rose in which the Divine Word became flesh; there are the lilies whose scent took the good path.
sentences.paradiso-23-s16: Thus Beatrice; and I, who was wholly ready for her counsels, again returned to the battle of my weak eyelids.
sentences.paradiso-23-s25: Then they remained there in my presence, singing 'Regina celi' so sweetly that the delight never left me.
sentences.paradiso-23-s26: Oh, how great is the abundance that lies enfolded in those very rich chests which were used to sow good bulbs here below!
sentences.paradiso-23-s27: There one lives and enjoys the treasure that was acquired by weeping in the exile of Babylon, where the gold was left.
sentences.paradiso-23-s28: There he triumphs under the lofty Son of God and of Mary, by his victory and with the ancient and with the new council, he who holds the keys of such glory.
sentences.paradiso-23-s1: Like a little bird among the beloved leaves, settled on the nest of its sweet young on the night that hides things from us — which, in order to see the longed-for faces and to find the food by which it may feed them (in which heavy toil they take pleasure), the season comes early upon an open bough, and with ardent longing awaits the sun, watching fixedly that the dawn may rise — so my lady stood upright and attentive, turned toward the region under which the sun shows less haste: so that, seeing her suspended and vague, she made me like one who, desiring, would wish for something else, and is satisfied while hoping.
sentences.paradiso-23-s2: But little time passed between one and another, I mean in my waiting and in my seeing the heavens coming more and more bright; and Beatrice said: “Behold the ranks of Christ’s triumph and all the fruit gathered from the turning of these spheres!”
sentences.paradiso-23-s4: It seemed to me that her whole face was afire, and her eyes were so full of joy that I must pass them by without artifice.
sentences.paradiso-23-s21: “I am angelic Love, who makes the lofty joy that breathes from the womb that was the lodging of our desire revolve; and I will go about, lady of Heaven, while you follow your Son, and you will cause the supreme sphere to give more room so that it may enter there.”
sentences.paradiso-23-s22: Thus the circling melody was sealed, and all the other lights made the name of Mary resound.
sentences.paradiso-23-s23: The royal mantle of all the volumes of the world — which grows more fervent and more alive in the breath of God and in human practice — lay above us, the inner shore so far away that its outline, where I was, had not yet appeared; therefore my eyes had not the power to follow the crowned flame that rose up beside its origin.
sentences.paradiso-23-s24: And as little children who, reaching toward their mother, stretch out their arms after they have taken the milk — because of the spirit that even kindles outwardly — each of those radiances stretched upward with its summit, so that the lofty affection they had for Mary became plain to me.
sentences.paradiso-23-s17: Like a sunbeam, which, now clear to my eyes through a rent cloud, already showed a meadow full of flowers, my eyes covered with shade; so I saw still greater throngs of splendors, struck from above by burning rays, without seeing the origin of the flashes.
sentences.paradiso-23-s18: O benign Power, who so impressed them, you exalted yourself on high, in order to grant me room for those eyes there which were not strong enough for you.
sentences.paradiso-23-s19: The name of the fair flower that I always invoke, both morning and evening, cramped my whole spirit into perceiving the greater fire; and as both lights painted for me the 'which' and the 'how much' of the living star that there above conquers as it conquered here below, a small torch descended within the heaven, shaped into a circle like a crown, and it girded and revolved around it.
sentences.paradiso-23-s20: Whatever melody more sweetly sounds here below and draws the soul more to itself would, compared with the sound of that lyre, seem a cloud that, torn, thunders — from which the fair sapphire was crowned, with which the clearer heaven is made more sapphire-colored.
